subConcepts.name: Volume of Cubes (edge x edge x edge)

learningGoal: How to find the volume of a cube by multiplying edge x edge x edge
Why all three dimensions are the same in a cube

screens.title: What's the volume of a 5cm cube?
screens.body:
Ruby has a cube-shaped jewellery box. Each edge is **5cm** long.
A cube is special — all three dimensions (measurements like length, width, and height) are the **same**! So the volume is just **5 × 5 × 5**.

screens.title: Edge x edge x edge!
screens.body: The cube has edges of **5cm**. Let's find the volume step by step. Tap to reveal:

steps.why: That's what the little ³ means — cubed!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: A cube has edges of **7cm**. What is the volume?

interaction.question: What is 7 × 7 × 7?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! **7³ = 343cm³**. That cube holds 343 tiny cubes inside! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 7 × 7 = 49, then × 7 = **343cm³**. Do it in two steps!

screens.title: Cube volume — triple the same number!
screens.body: A cube has all edges the same length, so:

learningGoal: Why cubing a number grows so fast
How to see that a small increase in edge length makes a big difference to volume

screens.title: One centimetre makes a BIG difference!
screens.body:
Ella is comparing a 3 cm cube and a 4 cm cube.
The 3cm cube has a volume of **27cm³**.
The 4cm cube has a volume of **64cm³**.
Just **1cm** bigger — but **37cm³** more volume!

screens.title: Cubing grows fast!
screens.body: When you **cube** a number, you multiply it by itself **three times**. Volume grows **much faster** than length — that's why a slightly bigger box holds a LOT more!

steps.why: A small edge increase = big volume increase!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is the volume of a cube with edges of **5cm**?

interaction.question: What is 5³?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 5 × 5 = 25, then × 5 = **125cm³**. Do it in two steps!

screens.title: Cubing = fast growth!
screens.body: Volume grows much faster than length because you're multiplying three times:

learningGoal: How to find a missing dimension (measurement) when you know the volume
Why dividing volume by the other two dimensions works

screens.title: Can you find the missing height?
screens.body:
Mrs Chen needs your help! The fish tank has a volume of 240 cm³. It is 10 cm long and 6 cm wide.
You know the volume and two dimensions. Can you work **backwards** to find the missing **height**?

screens.title: Divide volume by the known dimensions!

bodyParts.content: Here's how to find the missing **height**. Tap to reveal each step:

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Volume = **150cm³**. Two dimensions are **6cm** and **5cm**. What is the height?

screens.title: Finding a missing dimension!
screens.body: When you know the volume and two dimensions:

learningGoal: How to spot when someone uses the wrong operation for missing dimensions
Why subtraction doesn't work for finding missing dimensions

screens.title: Can you spot Ethan's mistake?
screens.body:
Ethan is finding the height of a box.
Volume = 180cm³. Dimensions: 9cm and 5cm.
Ethan says the height is **166cm**. Something's not right!

screens.title: Divide by BOTH known dimensions!
screens.body:
Ethan subtracted the dimensions from the volume (180 - 9 - 5 = 166) instead of dividing.

Correct method: multiply the two known dimensions first (9 × 5 = 45), then divide the volume by that.
**180 ÷ 45 = 4cm** ✓

steps.why: subtracted the dimensions from the volume (180 - 9 - 5 = 166) instead of dividing

screens.title: What's the correct answer?
screens.body: Volume = **288cm³**. Known dimensions: **8cm** and **6cm**. What is the height?

screens.title: Two common mistakes to avoid!
screens.body: When finding a missing dimension from volume:

subConcepts.name: Finding Edge Length from Cube Volume

learningGoal: How to find the edge length when you know a cube's volume
How to understand what a cube root (the number that multiplies by itself three times to give the volume) means

screens.title: What number × itself × itself = 27?
screens.body:
Maisie has a cube-shaped box with volume 27 cm³.
If it's a cube, all three edges are the **same length**. So what number, multiplied by itself three times, gives **27**?
This is called finding the **cube root**!

bodyParts.content: **Cubing** means multiplying a number by itself **three times** — like the three edges of a cube! The little ³ means "cubed".

Finding the **cube root** is the reverse — you ask: "What number cubed gives **27**?"

**3** × **3** × **3** = **27** — so the edge is **3cm**! ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: A cube has a volume of **64cm³**. What is the edge length?

interaction.question: What is the edge length?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 4 × 4 × 4 = 64, so the edge is **4cm**.

screens.title: Cube roots — the reverse of cubing!
screens.body: To find the edge of a cube from its volume, find the **cube root** — ask yourself: "What number × itself × itself gives this volume?"

learningGoal: How to remember the first ten cube numbers
Why knowing cube numbers helps in volume problems

screens.title: Know your cube numbers!
screens.body:
The first ten cube numbers are your **secret weapon** for volume questions:

**1, 8, 27, 64, 125, 216, 343, 512, 729, 1000**

If you spot one of these in a question, you can instantly find the edge length!

screens.title: Volume 64 — what's the edge?
screens.body:
A cube-shaped present has a volume of 64 cm³.
Look at the cube numbers list: **64** is there!
**4³ = 64**, so the edge is **4cm**.

Knowing your cube numbers makes these questions instant! ✓

screens.title: Quick! What's the edge?
screens.body: A cube has volume **125cm³**. What is the edge length?

interaction.question: What is the edge length?
interaction.correctFeedback: Quick thinking! **5³ = 125**, so the edge is **5cm**! ✓

screens.title: Cube numbers — learn them!
screens.body: Memorising cube numbers saves you loads of time in exams:

subConcepts.id: volume-to-capacity (how much a container can hold)
subConcepts.name: Converting cm³, ml, and litres

lessons.id: volume-to-capacity (how much a container can hold)-keyfact
learningGoal: How to convert between cm³, ml, and litres
How to convert between volume and capacity (how much a container can hold)

screens.title: The magic link: cm³ = ml!
screens.body:
Here's a brilliant fact:

**1 cm³ = 1 ml** (exactly!)
**1000 ml = 1 litre**

So **1000 cm³ = 1 litre**.

This means you can easily convert between volume (cm³) and capacity (litres)!

screens.title: 5000 cm³ = how many litres?
screens.body:
A fish tank has a volume of 5000 cm³.

The **capacity** (how much a container can hold) is the same as its volume. Since 1 cm³ = 1 ml, the tank holds **5000 ml**.
Since 1000 ml = 1 litre, that's **5000 ÷ 1000 = 5 litres**. ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
A container has a volume of **7000 cm³**. How many litres is that?

Remember: 1 cm³ = 1 ml, and 1000 ml = 1 litre.

interaction.question: How many litres is 7000 cm³?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 7000 cm³ = 7000 ml. Then ÷ 1000 = **7 litres**.

screens.title: Volume and capacity — the key conversions!
screens.body: Remember these three conversions:

steps.why: They're the exact same amount of space!

lessons.id: volume-to-capacity (how much a container can hold)-mistake
learningGoal: How to spot the ÷ 100 vs ÷ 1000 mistake
Why cm³ to litres uses ÷ 1000, not ÷ 100

screens.title: Can you spot Noah's mistake?
screens.body:
Noah is converting a fish tank volume of 8000 cm³ to litres.
Noah says: 8000 cm³ = **80 litres**.
That's 10 times too big! What went wrong?

screens.title: Divide by 1000, not 100!
screens.body:
Noah divided by 100 instead of 1000.

To convert volume to **capacity** (how much a container can hold) in litres: 1000 cm³ = 1 litre (not 100!). So:
**8000 ÷ 1000 = 8 litres** ✓

steps.why: Used ÷ 100 — that's for cm to m, not cm³ to litres!

screens.title: What's the correct answer?
screens.body:
Convert **6000 cm³** to litres.

Remember: 1000 cm³ = 1 litre (divide by 1000, not 100!)

interaction.question: How many litres is 6000 cm³?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 6000 ÷ 1000 = **6 litres**. Remember: divide by 1000!

screens.title: The conversion trap!
screens.body: Don't mix up your conversions:

steps.text: Remember: 1 cm³ = 1 ml, and 1000 ml = 1 litre

learningGoal: How to understand that two different cuboids can have the same volume
Why rearranging dimensions doesn't change the volume

bodyParts.content: Priya is comparing two different-shaped boxes that hold the same amount. They look completely different, but they have the **same volume**!

screens.title: Volume depends on ALL three dimensions!

bodyParts.content: As long as **l × w × h** gives the same answer, the volume is the same — even if the shapes look totally different!
Both equal **120** — same volume, different shapes! ✓
Here's a new box with different dimensions. What is its volume? Calculate to find out!

screens.title: Same volume, different shapes!
screens.body: Key things to remember about comparing volumes:

learningGoal: How to compare volumes quickly
Why you need to calculate, not just guess from appearance

bodyParts.content: Ben is choosing the bigger lunchbox. Which holds more? You can't tell just by looking — you need to **calculate**!

screens.title: What's the volume of this box?
screens.body: A new box: **9cm × 7cm × 6cm**

screens.title: Always calculate — never guess!
screens.body: When comparing volumes:

subConcepts.name: Doubling Edge Length Multiplies Volume by 8

learningGoal: How to understand that doubling every edge multiplies the volume by 8
Why doubling every edge makes the volume 8 times bigger, not 2 times

screens.title: Double the size — how much more volume?

bodyParts.content: Alfie is comparing a small cube and a double-sized cube. The volume didn't just double — it went up by **8 times**! Why?
When you **double** every edge, you're doubling **three things** — length, width, AND height. So the volume multiplier is **2 × 2 × 2 = 8**!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: A cube has edges of **4 cm** (volume = 64 cm³). If every edge is doubled, what is the new volume?

screens.title: The multiplication rule!
screens.body: When you make every edge of a 3D shape bigger:

learningGoal: How to spot the common mistake of multiplying by 2 instead of 8
Why doubling edges means multiplying volume by 8, not 2

screens.title: Can you spot Tom's mistake?
screens.body:
Tom is doubling the edges of a 4 cm cube. The original volume is 64cm³.
Tom says the new volume is **128cm³** (just doubled). Is that right?

bodyParts.content: Tom's answer: 64 × 2 = **128cm³** — but doubling all edges doesn't just double the volume...
Tom multiplied the volume by 2 (64 × 2 = 128) instead of by 8. When you double **all three edges**, you multiply the volume by **2 × 2 × 2 = 8**, not just 2!

screens.title: What's the correct new volume?
screens.body: A cube has volume **125 cm³**. All edges are doubled. What is the new volume?

interaction.question: What is the new volume?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 125 × 8 = **1000cm³**. Remember: double edges = × 8, not × 2!

screens.title: Don't fall for the × 2 trap!
screens.body: The exam loves this trick question. Remember:

subConcepts.name: Finding a Fraction of Total Volume

learningGoal: How to find a fraction of a volume
When you might need to find part of a total volume

screens.title: How much is three-quarters of 360?
screens.body:
A fish tank holds 360 cm³ of water. It is three-quarters full.
How much is actually in it?
To find a **fraction of a volume**, divide by the bottom number to find one part, then multiply by the top number!

screens.title: Divide then multiply!
screens.body: Finding **3/4** of **360 cm³** — same method as fractions of any amount!

steps.text: Step 1: Divide by the bottom number (4)
Step 2: Multiply by the top number (3)

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **3/4** of **400cm³**?

screens.title: Fraction of a volume!
screens.body: To find a fraction of any amount (including volume):

learningGoal: How to work through fraction-of-volume problems step by step
How to find the volume first, then the fraction

screens.title: Two-step challenge!
screens.body:
A cuboid container is 10 cm × 8 cm × 6 cm. It is half full of water.
First you need to find the total volume, THEN find the fraction of it. Let's go step by step!

screens.title: Step by step!
screens.body: First find the total volume — that's the full **capacity** (how much a container can hold). Then divide by the **denominator** (bottom number) and multiply by the **numerator** (top number). Tap to reveal each step:

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: The total volume is **360cm³**. What is **1/2** of 360?

screens.title: Volume + fractions — two steps!
screens.body: When a container is partly full:

learningGoal: How to spot when a word problem needs volume
How to pick out the right dimensions from a word problem

screens.title: When do you need volume?
screens.body:
A rectangular swimming pool is 12 m long, 5 m wide, and 2 m deep.
How much water does it hold?

When a question mentions **three dimensions** (length, width, height/depth) and asks about **how much it holds (3D space)**, it's a volume question!

screens.title: Multiply all three!
screens.body:
**Volume = length × width × height**
= 12 × 5 × 2
= **120m³** ✓

Look for words like: **holds**, **fills**, **capacity (how much a container can hold)**, **space inside**, **packed into**.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
A rectangular paddling pool is 6 m long, 4 m wide, and 1 m deep.
How much water does it hold?

interaction.question: What is 6 × 4 × 1?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 6 × 4 = 24, then × 1 = **24m³**. Do it in two steps!

screens.title: Spotting volume in word problems!
screens.body: Volume clue words in exam questions:

learningGoal: How to spot when someone confuses area and volume
Why you need all THREE dimensions for volume

screens.title: Can you spot Freya's mistake?
screens.body:
Freya is finding the volume of a box that is 7 cm long, 4 cm wide, and 3 cm tall.
Freya says the volume is **28cm³**. That's not right!

screens.title: Volume needs ALL THREE dimensions!
screens.body:
Freya only multiplied two dimensions (7 × 4 = 28) instead of all three — that's the area of the base, not the volume!.

Volume = length × width × **height**. You need all three!

Correct: 7 × 4 × 3 = **84cm³** ✓

steps.why: only multiplied two dimensions (7 × 4 = 28) instead of all three — that's the area of the base, not the volume!

screens.title: What's the correct volume?
screens.body: A cuboid is **6cm × 5cm × 4cm**. What is the volume?

screens.title: Volume vs area — don't mix them up!
screens.body: The biggest volume mistake is using only two dimensions:
